questions.text: I find that I am usually the one helping the group uphold its own standards.
I think that approaching tragic situations with composure and structure allows for more thorough and less biased problem-solving.
I tend to process surprising information with a slight pause rather than a physical jolt.
I find that I can sustain helpful engagement with people in pain over long periods because I do not take on their emotional state.
I see collaboration as a core part of my identity.
I hold the view that overthinking social signals leads to unnecessary confusion.
I create backup plans because I know that primary plans sometimes fail.
I resist routines because they make my days feel repetitive and dull.
I find that designing my ideal morning, without accommodation for anyone else, sets me up for success.
My emotional reactions are gradual — they build slowly rather than arriving all at once.
I pick up on the way someone's voice gets slightly higher when they are not fully confident in what they are saying.
I tend to set my thermostat, my music, and my lighting to what makes my household most comfortable, not just me.
I wake from nightmares with my heart pounding and the fear persisting long after I'm fully awake.
I find that keeping my personal goals front and center gives my life direction.
I hold the view that discipline is the bridge between goals and accomplishment.
I'm comfortable leaving things unfinished, trusting I'll return to them when the time is right.
I feel that my emotional thermostat is set higher than average.
I believe that maintaining my own standards of living is non-negotiable.
I prefer to personally handle the most high-stakes elements of any project.
I believe that the effort I put into making others successful is more valuable than the effort I put into my own success.
I pick up on which topics make certain people visibly tense.
I believe that quick wins build momentum that eventually creates sustainable results.
I find that the moral pull of another person's suffering has a directness that cuts through ambiguity and points me toward right action.
I feel that groups benefit from working through inefficiency at their own pace.
I feel most alive when I am steering the course of a project.
I rarely procrastinate because I tackle tasks as soon as they're identified.
I experience jealousy as a burning, twisting sensation that is impossible to intellectualize away.
I tend to be unaware of the relational dynamics underlying a group discussion.
I hold the view that the moral demand of suffering is strengthened, not weakened, by understanding the full context of what someone is enduring.
I detect when someone is subtly seeking validation in the way they tell a story.
I enjoy negotiating from a position of authority.
I tend not to read meaning into the way someone signs off an email.
I find that my emotional landscape is characterized by gentle slopes rather than cliffs.
I find that analytical detachment during emotional situations has allowed me to provide advice that people initially resisted but later appreciated for its soundness.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the insight that every ethical tradition worth respecting has recognized, however differently it may be expressed.
I routinely accept the worst option for myself so that others can have the better one.
